claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 5 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in approved education
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 16 and in approved education; Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 16 and in approved education
Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for.**

**How old is the child?**

Please let me know which category applies:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children you'd like to claim for, please tell me the ages (or age categories) of each child.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I have two children - Alex is 16 and Blake is 5 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's great to meet you. Let me help you check eligibility for both Alex (16 years old) and Blake (5 years old).

Since they're in different age categories, I'll assess them separately.

---

**Starting with Blake (5 years old):**

Since Blake is under 16, they automatically meet the age requirement. Let me check the next condition for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For Blake (age 5), I assume they're not working, so this should be straightforward. 

**Is Blake (age 5) working less than 24 hours per week, or not in paid work at all?**

(I expect the answer is yes since Blake is only 5, but I need to confirm to proceed correctly.)

---

Once we've gone through Blake's assessment, we'll do the same for Alex (16), who will have some additional questions since they're in the 16-19 age category.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Blake doesn't work at all, they're only 5!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! That makes perfect sense. Let me continue the assessment for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question for Blake:**

**Is Blake NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

This means Blake isn't personally claiming benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit. 

(Again, at age 5, I'd expect Blake isn't receiving these benefits, but I just need to confirm.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you for confirming. Let me continue with Blake's assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question for Blake:**

**Is Blake currently in an apprenticeship?**

At age 5, I'm assuming Blake is not in any apprenticeship, but just to confirm - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't in an apprenticeship - they're only 5!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Ha! You're absolutely right - I apologise for the silly question! Let me move on with Blake's assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now we need to check your residency status. This applies to you as the claimant (and will cover both children).

**Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?**

You only need to meet **ONE** of these:

1. **Normally live in the UK** - You usually reside in the UK
2. **Settled status** - You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Pre-settled status and working** - You have pre-settled status and are working/earning above the National Insurance threshold
4. **Pre-settled status and seeking work** - You have pre-settled status and are actively looking for work
5. **Pre-settled status with sufficient resources** - You have pre-settled status and can support yourself financially (not claiming certain benefits)
6. **Pre-settled status and studying** - You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. **Pre-settled status as family member** - You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. **Crown servant abroad** - You're living abroad as a Crown servant

Which of these applies to you? (For most people, option 1 - normally living in the UK - is the most common.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you! Living in the UK meets the residency requirement. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about your responsibility for the children:**

**Are you responsible for Alex and Blake?**

You meet this requirement if **any** of the following apply:

1. **Living with the children** - Alex and Blake live with you
2. **Contributing to their upkeep** - You pay at least the Child Benefit amount (or equivalent) towards their care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. **Paying maintenance** - You make maintenance payments under a court order or agreement

Which of these applies to your situation with Alex and Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives with me at home. Alex doesn't live with me, but I help with their upkeep - I contribute towards things like clothes and school supplies.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Both situations can qualify you for Child Benefit:

- **Blake** lives with you ✓
- **Alex** - you contribute to their upkeep ✓

Both meet the responsibility requirement. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important question about other claimants:**

**Is anyone else currently claiming (or planning to claim) Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- Someone else the child lives with

This matters because only one person can receive Child Benefit for a child at a time.

Please let me know the situation for each child:
- **Blake** (who lives with you)
- **Alex** (who doesn't live with you - is someone else claiming for them?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: For Alex, no one else is claiming for them. But for Blake, their other parent claims Child Benefit and Blake lives with them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that important clarification. Let me make sure I understand correctly:

**Blake's situation:** You mentioned earlier that Blake lives with you, but now you've said Blake lives with their other parent who claims Child Benefit for them. Could you clarify - does Blake live with you, with their other parent, or split time between both homes?

**For Alex:** No one else is claiming - that's clear. ✓

Understanding Blake's living situation will help me determine the correct eligibility path. If Blake primarily lives with their other parent who already claims Child Benefit, that would affect your eligibility for Blake.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Sorry, let me clarify - Blake lives with their other parent, not with me. Their other parent is the one who claims Child Benefit for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
No problem, thank you for clarifying! That's an important distinction.

So to summarise:

- **Blake (age 5):** Lives with their other parent, who already claims Child Benefit for them
- **Alex (age 16):** Doesn't live with you, but you contribute to their upkeep, and no one else is claiming for them

**For Blake:** Since Blake lives with their other parent and that parent already claims Child Benefit, you wouldn't be able to claim for Blake. Only one person can receive Child Benefit for a child, and priority is usually given to the person the child lives with.

**Let's now focus on Alex (age 16):**

Since Alex is 16, I need to go back and check the specific requirements for their age group.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now for Alex (age 16):**

Since Alex is between 16 and 19, there are additional requirements. 

**Is Alex currently:**

1. **In education or training** - such as school, college, or an approved training course?
2. **Recently left education** - within the last 20 weeks?
3. **Neither** - not in education/training and hasn't recently left?

Which of these applies to Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is in education - they're studying for their A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, A-levels is a common qualification for 16-year-olds. Let me check the education requirements for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent news! **A-levels are on the approved list** for Child Benefit eligibility. 

I just need to confirm a couple of things about Alex's studies:

1. **Is Alex studying full-time?** (This means at least 12 hours per week of supervised study)

2. **Is the course funded by an employer** or linked to a guaranteed job with an employer afterwards?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is studying full-time at sixth form. It's not employer-funded - just regular A-levels at school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! That's exactly what we need to hear. A-levels at sixth form, full-time and not employer-funded - that all qualifies as approved education. ✓

Now let me continue checking the remaining requirements for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question for Alex:**

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not working at all)?**

This is about any paid work Alex might do alongside their studies.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all - they just focus on their A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that's straightforward then. Let me continue with Alex's assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question for Alex:**

**Is Alex NOT receiving any benefits in their own name?**

This means Alex isn't personally claiming benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question for Alex:**

**Is Alex in an apprenticeship?**

Since you mentioned Alex is studying A-levels at sixth form, I assume they're not in an apprenticeship - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - Alex isn't in an apprenticeship, just doing A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: You've already told me you live in the UK (in Manchester), so the residency requirement is met. ✓

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: You've also confirmed you contribute to Alex's upkeep (clothes, school supplies, etc.), so the responsibility requirement is met. ✓

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: And you confirmed no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. ✓

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about Alex's living situation:**

**Where does Alex currently live?** Is Alex:

1. **In hospital** - staying in hospital for a period of time
2. **In local authority care** - looked after by the council
3. **Fostered** - living with foster parents
4. **Normal living situation** - living at home (with their other parent, family, etc.) or in ordinary accommodation

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is in a normal living situation - they live with their other parent.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
We're almost there! One final question for Alex:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, Alex is living in a normal family situation without any council funding for their care - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - the council isn't paying anything towards Alex's care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That completes the assessment for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I now have all the information I need to give you a final outcome. Let me summarise:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Assessment Complete!

Here's your Child Benefit eligibility summary:

### ✅ Alex (age 16) - **ELIGIBLE**
You **can** claim Child Benefit for Alex because:
- Alex is 16 and in approved full-time education (A-levels at sixth form)
- The course is not employer-funded
- Alex isn't working 24+ hours per week
- Alex doesn't receive benefits in their own name
- Alex isn't in a disqualifying apprenticeship
- You live in the UK (Manchester)
- You contribute to Alex's upkeep (clothes, school supplies)
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- The local council isn't paying for Alex's care

### ❌ Blake (age 5) - **NOT ELIGIBLE** (for your claim)
You **cannot** claim Child Benefit for Blake because:
- Blake lives with their other parent
- Their other parent already claims Child Benefit for Blake
- Only one person can receive Child Benefit per child, and priority goes to the person the child lives with

---

## Next Steps for Alex

To claim Child Benefit for Alex, you should:

1. **Apply online** at [gov.uk/child-benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim) or by post using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** - if you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may need to pay some or all of the benefit back through tax
5. **Payments** are usually made every 4 weeks

Is there anything else you'd like to know about claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

reasonings: Alex (age 16) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) They are in the 16-19 age group and enrolled in approved non-advanced education (A-levels at sixth form, full-time, not employer-funded); (2) They are not working 24+ hours per week; (3) They don't receive benefits in their own name; (4) They are not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) You meet the UK residency requirement (living in Manchester); (6) You are responsible for Alex by contributing to their upkeep (clothes, school supplies); (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex is in a normal living situation; and (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's care.

Blake (age 5) does not qualify for YOUR Child Benefit claim because: Although Blake meets the age requirement (under 16), Blake lives with their other parent who already claims Child Benefit for them. Only one person can receive Child Benefit for a child at a time, and priority is given to the person the child lives with. Since Blake lives with their other parent and that parent is already claiming, you cannot make a separate claim for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex, but not for Blake. Alex meets all the requirements as a 16-year-old in approved full-time education (A-levels), and you qualify as the claimant through your contributions to Alex's upkeep. For Blake, since they live with their other parent who already claims Child Benefit, you cannot make a claim for Blake.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 16) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) They are in the 16-19 age group and enrolled in approved non-advanced education (A-levels at sixth form, full-time, not employer-funded); (2) They are not working 24+ hours per week; (3) They don't receive benefits in their own name; (4) They are not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) You meet the UK residency requirement (living in Manchester); (6) You are responsible for Alex by contributing to their upkeep (clothes, school supplies); (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex is in a normal living situation; and (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's care.

Blake (age 5) does not qualify for YOUR Child Benefit claim because: Although Blake meets the age requirement (under 16), Blake lives with their other parent who already claims Child Benefit for them. Only one person can receive Child Benefit for a child at a time, and priority is given to the person the child lives with. Since Blake lives with their other parent and that parent is already claiming, you cannot make a separate claim for Blake.

response.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex, but not for Blake. Alex meets all the requirements as a 16-year-old in approved full-time education (A-levels), and you qualify as the claimant through your contributions to Alex's upkeep. For Blake, since they live with their other parent who already claims Child Benefit, you cannot make a claim for Blake.
